title: Community Reclamation Partnerships Act of 2025
plain_summary: This law helps clean up old coal mines that were abandoned before 1977 and left behind polluted land and water. It lets states work with federal agencies and private volunteer groups called Community Reclaimers to fix these problems. The volunteer groups must be people who did not cause the pollution in the first place and have not broken any mining rules. This program will run until 2032 to help restore damaged areas from old coal mining.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Community Reclamation Partnerships Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Reference
sections.text: Unless clearly stated otherwise, whenever this law mentions changing a part of another law, it is talking about changing a part of the Surface Mining Control and Reclamation Act of nineteen seventy seven.

sections.title: Section 3 State Memoranda of Understanding for Certain Remediation
sections.text: This section adds new rules to an existing law about cleaning up old mine sites. It creates two new programs. First, states can make formal agreements with federal agencies to clean up water pollution from old mines. These agreements must have a plan to improve water quality, include monitoring and reporting, cover operation and maintenance of treatment systems, and address other needs. Before making these agreements, states must let the public comment and hold at least one public meeting with fifteen days advance notice in local newspapers and online. The state must respond to public concerns. The Secretary and Environmental Protection Agency Administrator have one hundred twenty days to approve or reject these agreements. Second, the law creates Community Reclaimer partnerships where private groups can help clean up old mine sites. A Community Reclaimer is any person or company that wants to help with cleanup, did not cause the original pollution, and has no outstanding violations. The Secretary has one hundred twenty days to approve projects if they meet all requirements. States must take responsibility for costs and damages except when caused by gross negligence or intentional wrongdoing. Projects must be on sites already inventoried as needing cleanup, follow state agreements for water pollution projects, and meet detailed submission requirements including engineering plans, cost estimates, schedules, and contingency plans. Community Reclaimers can reprocess old mine materials only if approved by land management agencies and if proceeds help pay for cleanup costs.

sections.title: Section 4 Clarifying State Liability for Mine Drainage Projects
sections.text: This section changes existing law to say that states will not be held responsible under federal water pollution laws for mine drainage treatment projects if those projects follow approved state agreements made under the new rules in this law.

sections.title: Section 5 Conforming Amendments
sections.text: This section updates an existing list in the law to include the new Community Reclaimer projects created by this law.

sections.title: Section 6 Sunset Provision
sections.text: This law will stop working on September thirtieth, two thousand thirty two.
